title: Grain Assertion Before Aggregation
when_to_use: user wants to GROUP BY a composite key but fears duplicated rows inflating aggregates
after a JOIN, verifying that no row multiplication occurred (fan-out detection)
before computing totals per customer/order/product to catch silent duplication
building alerts that surface unexpected duplicates in critical dimensions
validating that a business key (e.g., order_id, user_id) is truly unique before aggregation
auditing ETL pipeline output for grain violations
when_not_to_use: when the grain is intentionally many-to-one and duplicates are expected (e.g., order_items per order) — use grain assertion only for dimension tables
when the table is known to have duplicates but the aggregation handles them (e.g., using SUM instead of COUNT) — assertion adds unnecessary overhead
for high-cardinality keys where duplicates are rare and expensive to check at scale — consider spot-checks instead
when counting distinct values (e.g., unique users) — the grain is already implicit; use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT instead
when validating foreign key integrity — use dq_referential_integrity instead
for time-sensitive alerts where speed trumps completeness — use sampling or approximate dedup checks

concept_explanation.what_it_does: A grain assertion verifies that a key or set of keys identifies exactly one row — by comparing COUNT(*) to COUNT(DISTINCT key) — before using the data in an aggregation. It catches silent duplication that would cause aggregates like SUM or COUNT to be inflated.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine checking that each SKU in your product table appears only once. If COUNT(*) > COUNT(DISTINCT sku), then multiple rows share the same SKU, meaning your 'per-SKU' aggregation will be wrong. This is a data quality gate before analysis.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: The assertion only catches *within-table* duplication. If duplication arises from a JOIN, it will not be detected here — use dq_row_count_assertion or join_fanout_detection instead.

template.minimal:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS row_count, COUNT(DISTINCT sku) AS distinct_sku FROM products;
SELECT CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = COUNT(DISTINCT sku) THEN 'OK' ELSE 'GRAIN VIOLATION' END AS grain_status FROM products;
template.realistic:
-- Verify that order_id is unique in the orders table before joining to order_items
WITH grain_check AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS unique_orders,
    COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS duplicate_count
  FROM orders
)
SELECT
  total_rows,
  unique_orders,
  duplicate_count,
  CASE
    WHEN duplicate_count = 0 THEN 'PASS: grain is correct'
    ELSE 'FAIL: ' || duplicate_count || ' duplicate order_ids found'
  END AS grain_status
FROM grain_check;
template.composable:
-- Grain check + safe join (combines dq_grain_assertion + dq_safe_division for downstream metrics)
-- Composes: dq_grain_assertion + dq_safe_division
WITH order_grain AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_orders,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS unique_orders
  FROM orders
),
orders_unique AS (
  SELECT * FROM orders
  WHERE order_id IN (SELECT order_id FROM order_grain WHERE total_orders = unique_orders)
),
order_totals AS (
  SELECT
    o.order_id,
    COUNT(oi.id) AS item_count,
    SUM(oi.amount) AS order_amount
  FROM orders_unique o
  JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
  GROUP BY 1
)
SELECT
  order_id,
  item_count,
  order_amount,
  ROUND(order_amount / NULLIF(item_count, 0), 2) AS avg_item_price
FROM order_totals
WHERE item_count > 0

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using COUNT(DISTINCT key) alone without row count comparison
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: COUNT(DISTINCT) masks the duplication — it returns the correct number of unique keys but hides that duplicates exist. Aggregations on the table will still be wrong because SUM() and COUNT() act on all rows, not just distinct keys.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Aggregation results (e.g., total revenue) are unexpectedly high; comparing against source systems shows mismatches only after joins.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS unique_orders FROM orders;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS unique, COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS dupes FROM orders WHERE dupes > 0;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS duplicate_rows FROM orders;

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: A table may be grain-perfect in the source, but transformations (e.g., unpivoting, deduping with wrong key) can break the grain. Without rechecking after transformation, the error propagates silently.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Reports are correct for raw tables but wrong for processed views or CTEs.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: WITH orders_dedup AS (SELECT DISTINCT * FROM orders) SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders_dedup;
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
WITH orders_dedup AS (
  SELECT order_id, MAX(created_at) AS created_at, COUNT(*) AS dupe_count
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY 1
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS problem_rows FROM orders_dedup;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Checking grain on only part of a composite key
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: The grain may be defined by (customer_id, month), but checking only customer_id returns 'OK' even though multiple months per customer create duplicate rows at the customer grain.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Monthly aggregations per customer show duplicated revenue, but per-customer-only check passed.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) = COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS ok FROM monthly_revenue;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) = COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id || '_' || month) AS ok FROM monthly_revenue;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT customer_id, month, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM monthly_revenue GROUP BY 1,2 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: COUNT(DISTINCT key) ignores NULLs — if key has NULLs, they’re excluded from both numerator and denominator, making duplication checks deceptive.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same: NULLs are excluded from COUNT(DISTINCT).
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same behavior. However, BigQuery COUNT(DISTINCT) is approximate by default for large cardinals unless ROUNDING_MODE=EXACT.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Before assertion, explicitly filter or handle NULLs: COUNT(DISTINCT COALESCE(key, 'NULL_SENTINEL')) to avoid silent exclusions.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: string concatenation (e.g., col1 || '_' || col2) works, but special characters in data can cause collisions (e.g., 'a_b_c' vs 'a_b' + '_c').
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Supports CONCAT(col1, col2, ...) or ||, but same collision risk.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use struct-like comparison: COUNT(DISTINCT STRUCT(col1, col2)) in BigQuery, or use a hash: COUNT(DISTINCT MD5(CAST(row AS TEXT))) in DuckDB/Postgres for safety.

choose_this_when: user wants to ensure a dimension table has no duplicate primary keys before analysis
building automated data quality tests for ETL pipelines
validating JOIN inputs to prevent row multiplication
auditing new database columns or changes to uniqueness constraints
spot-checking before high-stakes reporting (e.g., quarterly revenue)

choose_alternative_when.condition: user suspects fan-out from JOIN, not within-table duplication
choose_alternative_when.why: This pattern checks table-level uniqueness; dq_row_count_assertion compares row counts before/after JOIN

choose_alternative_when.condition: user needs to deduplicate data, not just detect issues
choose_alternative_when.why: Assertion identifies the problem; idempotent_dedup resolves it deterministically

choose_alternative_when.condition: user is verifying foreign key integrity (e.g., 'every order_id in order_items exists in orders')
choose_alternative_when.why: Grain checks validate internal consistency, not cross-table reference validity

decision_phrase_triggers: can I GROUP BY this safely?

edge_cases.scenario: NULLs in key columns (e.g., optional foreign key)
edge_cases.what_happens: COUNT(DISTINCT) ignores NULLs, so duplicates with NULLs are not counted — grain appears valid even if non-NULL duplicates exist.
edge_cases.handling: Filter out NULLs before assertion or treat them explicitly: COUNT(DISTINCT COALESCE(key, '__NULL__')) > 0.

edge_cases.scenario: Natural keys with high entropy (e.g., UUIDs)
edge_cases.what_happens: COUNT(DISTINCT) and COUNT(*) are nearly identical even with duplicates — the difference is negligible, making assertions less informative.
edge_cases.handling: For UUIDs, use a hash of other attributes (e.g., MD5(data_columns)) to detect logical duplicates rather than relying on ID.

edge_cases.scenario: Temporal tables with versioned rows (e.g., effective dates)
edge_cases.what_happens: The primary key (e.g., user_id) is duplicated across versions — but this is expected and correct. Grain assertion fails incorrectly.
edge_cases.handling: Check grain only on (key, version_column) or use valid_from/valid_to ranges to define effective grain.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) for scan + O(n log n) for DISTINCT sort
performance_notes.when_slow: High-cardinality keys (>100M rows, >10M distinct) cause excessive disk spilling. Slower when table is large and unindexed.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter to relevant date range or status before assertion. For large tables, use approximate COUNT(DISTINCT) (e.g., APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT in BigQuery) — trade some accuracy for speed. In DuckDB, add indexes on the key column.

title: Safe Division
when_to_use: user needs to compute a ratio or percentage where denominator may be zero or NULL
calculating averages, rates, or percentages across user segments or time periods
aggregating metrics where group size might be zero (e.g., conversions with no impressions)
avoiding NaN or Infinity in downstream BI tools or ML models
dividing two aggregations that come from different sources (e.g., sales / target)
when_not_to_use: when denominator is guaranteed non-zero (e.g., row count in a table with >0 rows) — NULLIF adds unnecessary complexity
when zero should be treated as an error condition and the query should fail — use explicit validation instead
in JOINs where denominator is from a right table with no matching rows — consider using LEFT JOIN + COALESCE or excluding with WHERE instead
when computing a log or other non-linear transform — division is just the first step; use NULLIF inside the transform instead
for boolean flags or existence checks — division is overkill; use EXISTS or COUNT(*) > 0 instead

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Safe division prevents division-by-zero errors and propagating NULLs by wrapping the denominator in NULLIF(..., 0) or handling NULL with COALESCE. This ensures the query returns a defined numeric result (e.g., 0 or NULL) instead of crashing or producing NaN.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Divide by zero is like asking 'how many apples per zero people?' — it's undefined. NULLIF(denominator, 0) converts zero to NULL, and dividing any number by NULL yields NULL — a safe, intentional result. COALESCE lets you replace NULL with 0 or a default if needed.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: NULLIF(denominator, 0) only catches zeros — it does not catch NULLs in the denominator. Use COALESCE(denominator, 0) or NULLIF(COALESCE(denominator, 0), 0) for full coverage.

template.realistic:
-- Conversion rate per campaign with safe denominator handling
SELECT
  campaign_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS impressions,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN conversion = 1 THEN user_id END) AS conversions,
  ROUND(
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN conversion = 1 THEN user_id END)
    / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id), 0) * 100,
  2) AS conversion_rate
FROM campaigns
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY conversion_rate DESC NULLS LAST;
template.composable:
-- Safe division inside a CASE WHEN classification (combines dq_safe_division + seg_case_when_classification)
-- Composes: dq_safe_division + seg_case_when_classification
WITH campaign_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    campaign_id,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS impressions,
    COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN conversion = 1 THEN user_id END) AS conversions,
    SUM(CASE WHEN conversion = 1 THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) AS revenue
  FROM campaigns
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_rate AS (
  SELECT
    campaign_id,
    impressions,
    conversions,
    revenue,
    revenue / NULLIF(impressions, 0) AS revenue_per_impression
  FROM campaign_metrics
)
SELECT
  campaign_id,
  impressions,
  conversions,
  revenue_per_impression,
  CASE
    WHEN impressions = 0 THEN 'no_data'
    WHEN revenue_per_impression > 0.5 THEN 'high_value'
    WHEN revenue_per_impression > 0.1 THEN 'medium_value'
    ELSE 'low_value'
  END AS campaign_tier
FROM with_rate
ORDER BY revenue_per_impression DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using NULLIF only on denominator but not handling NULL in numerator
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If numerator is NULL (e.g., no conversions), the division becomes NULL / 0 → NULL (safe), but if denominator is also NULL, NULL / NULL → NULL — ambiguous. More critically, NULL in numerator causes entire expression to be NULL, hiding potential zeros.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Conversion rate shows NULL instead of 0% for campaigns with zero conversions, confusing business users who expect '0%' for inactive campaigns.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT conversions / NULLIF(impressions, 0) AS rate FROM campaign_summary;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COALESCE(conversions, 0) / NULLIF(impressions, 0) AS rate FROM campaign_summary;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM campaign_summary WHERE conversions IS NULL;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using COALESCE on numerator but not denominator in division
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If denominator is 0, division crashes. COALESCE(numerator, 0) doesn’t protect against denominator = 0.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query fails with 'division by zero' error at runtime.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT COALESCE(conversions, 0) / impressions AS rate FROM campaign_summary;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT COALESCE(conversions, 0) / NULLIF(impressions, 0) AS rate FROM campaign_summary;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM campaign_summary WHERE impressions = 0;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Confusing NULLIF vs COALESCE roles — using COALESCE on denominator only
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: COALESCE(denominator, 0) turns NULL to 0, but 0 in the denominator still triggers division by zero. NULLIF must wrap the zero-converted denominator.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query throws 'division by zero' when denominator is NULL, despite COALESCE.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT numerator / COALESCE(denominator, 0) AS rate FROM data;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT numerator / NULLIF(COALESCE(denominator, 0), 0) AS rate FROM data;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM data WHERE denominator IS NULL OR denominator = 0;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: NULLIF(denom, 0) works exactly as expected — returns NULL if denom = 0, otherwise returns denom.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same behavior — NULLIF is standard SQL and supported identically.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: NULLIF works identically, but BigQuery coerces types strictly — mixing INT and FLOAT may cause error; cast to same type first.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always cast both to same type: NULLIF(CAST(denom AS FLOAT), 0). This avoids subtle coercion errors.

dialect_traps.construct: Division with NULL semantics across dialects
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: Any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL — no surprise. Safe division with NULLIF ensures only zeros trigger NULL, not NULLs in data.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same NULL behavior. However, PostgreSQL's strong typing may throw 'operator is not unique' errors with mixed numeric types; explicit CAST helps.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same NULL rules, but BigQuery silently coerces to FLOAT in division — e.g., 5/2 = 2.5 — unlike DuckDB where 5/2 = 2 (integer division) if both are INT.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Cast numerators and denominators to FLOAT before division to ensure non-integer results are expected: CAST(numerator AS FLOAT) / NULLIF(CAST(denominator AS FLOAT), 0).

choose_this_when: user asks 'how do I handle division when denominator might be zero?'
building formulas for rates, ratios, or percentages where denominator could be empty
output must be machine-readable (e.g., for ML models) without NaN/Inf
debugging division errors in existing queries — NULLIF is the most common fix

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants to skip rows where denominator is zero entirely
choose_alternative_when.why: Safe division returns 0 or NULL for problematic rows; CASE WHEN lets you exclude them with WHERE instead

choose_alternative_when.condition: zero denominator should never occur — it indicates a data or logic error
choose_alternative_when.why: Instead of masking the error, raise it explicitly with CASE WHEN to halt or alert

choose_alternative_when.condition: user needs to handle negative denominators or custom thresholds
choose_alternative_when.why: NULLIF only catches zeros; CASE WHEN allows arbitrary logic like NULLIF(denom, 0) AND denom < 0

decision_phrase_triggers: how to get rate when denominator is zero

edge_cases.scenario: Both numerator and denominator are zero
edge_cases.what_happens: NULLIF(0,0) returns NULL → 0 / NULL = NULL. Some users expect 0%, others expect 100% or NaN.
edge_cases.handling: Define business logic: use COALESCE(..., 0) to default to 0%, or add explicit CASE WHEN to handle 0/0 as 0%, 100%, or error.

edge_cases.scenario: Numerator is negative, denominator is positive
edge_cases.what_happens: Division yields a negative rate — mathematically correct, but may break downstream logic expecting non-negative percentages.
edge_cases.handling: Use ABS() on numerator if direction doesn’t matter: ABS(numerator) / NULLIF(denominator, 0). Or add CASE WHEN to cap rates.

edge_cases.scenario: Numerator and denominator come from LEFT JOIN with no match
edge_cases.what_happens: Both numerator and denominator may be NULL → division yields NULL. But business may want 0% or 0 numerator.
edge_cases.handling: Wrap both with COALESCE: COALESCE(numerator, 0) / NULLIF(COALESCE(denominator, 0), 0). Confirm with data: check if NULLs mean 'no events' or 'error'.

performance_notes.complexity: O(1) per row — trivial cost; NULLIF is a simple conditional check
performance_notes.when_slow: Only relevant when denominator is derived from a complex subquery or aggregation — the cost is in the subquery, not safe division itself.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: If division is done millions of times, precompute safe_ratio in a materialized view or CTE. Otherwise, it's negligible overhead. Avoid repeated NULLIF calls — compute once and reuse.

title: NULL-Safe Comparison
when_to_use: user wants to compare two columns that may contain NULLs and treat NULL=NULL as TRUE
JOINing or filtering on columns where NULLs should match (e.g., matching NULL dimensions)
checking if two values are equal without NULL tripping — especially in UPDATE or MERGE
implementing deduplication that treats two NULL values as the same entity
writing business rules where NULL is a valid value (e.g., status='NULL' means unassigned)
when_not_to_use: when NULLs should *not* be considered equal — standard SQL treats NULL != NULL, and that’s correct for most use cases (e.g., distinct values in COUNT(DISTINCT))
for performance-critical queries where COALESCE with placeholder values is cheaper than IS NOT DISTINCT FROM — test execution plans
when working with databases that don’t support IS NOT DISTINCT FROM (e.g., older Postgres versions) — use COALESCE alternatives
when comparing NULL to a non-NULL constant — simply use IS NULL instead
for boolean logic where NULL means 'unknown' and you want three-valued evaluation — IS NOT DISTINCT FROM bypasses three-valued logic

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Standard SQL equality (=) returns NULL (unknown) when either operand is NULL — never TRUE or FALSE. IS NOT DISTINCT FROM treats two NULLs as equal (TRUE) and NULL ≠ non-NULL (FALSE), aligning with human intuition. It resolves three-valued logic surprises.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Think of NULL as 'missing value'. Is a missing value equal to another missing value? IS NOT DISTINCT FROM says 'yes' — two unknowns are still the same unknown. Standard = says 'we can’t know, so return NULL'.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: IS NOT DISTINCT FROM is standard SQL-92 but not universally supported. DuckDB and Postgres support it natively; BigQuery does not — use COALESCE alternatives there.

template.minimal:
SELECT a IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b AS null_safe_equal FROM data;
-- For filtering: SELECT * FROM data WHERE a IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b;
template.realistic:
-- Match customers by email and state, treating NULL state as same (e.g., 'unknown')
WITH customer_pairs AS (
  SELECT
    c1.id AS c1_id,
    c2.id AS c2_id,
    c1.email,
    c1.state AS state1,
    c2.state AS state2
  FROM customers c1
  JOIN customers c2 ON c1.id < c2.id
  WHERE c1.email = c2.email
)
SELECT * FROM customer_pairs
WHERE state1 IS NOT DISTINCT FROM state2;
-- Equivalent without IS NOT DISTINCT FROM (portable):
SELECT * FROM customer_pairs
WHERE (state1 = state2) OR (state1 IS NULL AND state2 IS NULL);
template.composable:
-- Deduplication with null-safe key matching (combines dq_null_safe_comparison + dq_idempotent_dedup)
-- Composes: dq_null_safe_comparison + dq_idempotent_dedup
WITH customers_with_row AS (
  SELECT *,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY email,
        state IS NOT DISTINCT FROM 'NY'
      ORDER BY created_at DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM customers
)
SELECT * EXCEPT(rn)
FROM customers_with_row
WHERE rn = 1;
-- Explanation: Treats all 'NY' states as equal and all NULL states as equal, ensuring NULLs don't cause spurious duplicates.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using = to compare columns that may be NULL
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: When both columns are NULL, a = b returns NULL (not TRUE), so the WHERE clause filters it out. The row disappears even though logically both are missing and should match.
mistake_patterns.symptom: JOIN or filter returns fewer rows than expected; NULL values are silently dropped.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.status = t2.status;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.status IS NOT DISTINCT FROM t2.status;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.status = t2.status WHERE t1.status IS NULL AND t2.status IS NULL; -- Returns 0 if = is used
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.status IS NOT DISTINCT FROM t2.status WHERE t1.status IS NULL AND t2.status IS NULL; -- Returns count of matching NULLs

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using COALESCE with a sentinel value incorrectly
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: COALESCE(status, 'UNKNOWN') = COALESCE(status2, 'UNKNOWN') works, but if 'UNKNOWN' exists as a real value in the data, it collides — e.g., status='UNKNOWN' vs status=NULL both become 'UNKNOWN'.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Rows with status='UNKNOWN' incorrectly match NULL rows — false positives.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON COALESCE(t1.status, 'UNKNOWN') = COALESCE(t2.status, 'UNKNOWN');
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT status FROM t1 UNION ALL SELECT status FROM t2) GROUP BY 1 HAVING status = 'UNKNOWN';

mistake_patterns.mistake: Assuming NULLIF solves comparison — using NULLIF in WHERE
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: NULLIF(col, value) returns NULL if col=value — it does not help equality. It’s designed for division, not comparison.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Query returns no rows or crashes — NULLIF doesn’t address NULL comparison.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON NULLIF(t1.status, t2.status) IS NULL;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.status IS NOT DISTINCT FROM t2.status;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON NULLIF(t1.status, t2.status) IS NULL; -- Runs but returns 0 rows if any NULLs exist

dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Fully supported since version 8.4. Recommended for portability.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Not supported — throws 'Function IS NOT DISTINCT FROM not found' error.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use (a = b) OR (a IS NULL AND b IS NULL). This works everywhere, though verbose. For multiple columns, wrap in CASE or CTE.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: IS DISTINCT FROM returns TRUE if values differ *or* one is NULL and the other not. Its negation (IS NOT DISTINCT FROM) matches standard = for non-NULLs and treats NULL=NULL.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same behavior — IS DISTINCT FROM is supported and inverses IS NOT DISTINCT FROM.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Not supported — use CASE WHEN or COALESCE workarounds.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: IS NOT DISTINCT FROM = NOT (a IS DISTINCT FROM b). Prefer IS NOT DISTINCT FROM for clarity. Avoid IS DISTINCT FROM to reduce confusion.

choose_this_when: user says 'NULL should match NULL' in a business rule
building deduplication logic that treats missing values as identical
JOINing tables where NULL in dimension columns means the same (e.g., 'not assigned' or 'unknown')
debugging why rows disappear when comparing NULL columns

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants NULL and non-NULL to remain unequal — standard SQL behavior is correct
choose_alternative_when.why: IS NOT DISTINCT FROM *forces* NULL=TRUE; CASE WHEN preserves standard semantics and adds explicit handling

choose_alternative_when.condition: database lacks IS NOT DISTINCT FROM support (e.g., Redshift older versions)
choose_alternative_when.why: Portable COALESCE or explicit NULL checks work in all dialects

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants to filter out NULLs entirely before comparison
choose_alternative_when.why: WHERE col IS NOT NULL first avoids the problem entirely — simpler than null-safe comparison

edge_cases.scenario: Three columns with NULLs: a, b, c
edge_cases.what_happens: a IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b IS NOT DISTINCT FROM c requires pairwise checks — (a IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b) AND (b IS NOT DISTINCT FROM c).
edge_cases.handling: Chain comparisons: WHERE a IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b AND b IS NOT DISTINCT FROM c. For many columns, use ARRAY comparison (e.g., ARRAY[a,b] IS NOT DISTINCT FROM ARRAY[b,c]) if supported.

edge_cases.scenario: LEFT JOIN with NULL-safe ON clause
edge_cases.what_happens: Rows with NULL in joined column are included — not filtered out. This preserves 'unknown' matches, but may inflate row counts if multiple NULLs exist.
edge_cases.handling: Combine with aggregation or use a tiebreaker (e.g., ORDER BY id) to ensure deterministic matches.

edge_cases.scenario: Indexes don’t support IS NOT DISTINCT FROM
edge_cases.what_happens: Query may fall back to full scan — NULL-safe comparison can’t use standard B-tree indexes on equality.
edge_cases.handling: Create a functional index (e.g., COALESCE(col, 'NULL_SENTINEL')) if comparison is frequent. Or add a computed column with NULLs replaced.

performance_notes.complexity: O(1) per row — IS NOT DISTINCT FROM is a simple conditional check
performance_notes.when_slow: When applied to large joined tables without indexes, or when used in nested subqueries with correlated columns.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-compute null-safe comparisons in a CTE or materialized column. For frequent comparisons, replace IS NOT DISTINCT FROM with a sentinel column: UPDATE t SET status_clean = COALESCE(status, 'NULL_VALUE'). Index status_clean.

title: Time Filter Before Window Function
when_to_use: user needs to compute window functions (e.g., LAG, SUM OVER) but must exclude outliers, test data, or irrelevant time periods first
ensuring window frame uses only valid events (e.g., active users, non-test orders)
preventing data from a different period (e.g., 2025 test data) from polluting window calculations for 2024
avoiding performance issues by filtering before expensive window sorts
when_not_to_use: when filtering *should* happen after window evaluation (e.g., QUALIFY WHERE row_number = 1 after ranking)
when the window function itself defines the filter (e.g., using FILTER clause in aggregation)
when data quality issues mean filtering is redundant — use dq_grain_assertion instead
for time-insensitive windows (e.g., partition by user_id only, no time ordering)
when aggregating over entire history (e.g., total lifetime revenue) — time filter changes the domain

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Window functions compute over the *entire* table unless filtered first. Applying time filters (WHERE, QUALIFY) after the window causes calculations to include rows that should be excluded, resulting in incorrect baselines (e.g., LAG looks at last row including test data). Filtering first ensures windows operate on clean, relevant data.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Think of a window function as a SQL 'lens' — if you put it over a messy dataset and then clean the dataset, the lens has already distorted the view. Filter *before* applying the lens to keep the lens on clean data.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: In DuckDB, QUALIFY filters *after* window evaluation — if used for time filtering, it includes all rows in the window calculation, not just the filtered ones. Always use a CTE or WHERE before the window.

template.minimal:
WITH filtered AS (
  SELECT * FROM events WHERE event_date >= '2024-01-01'
)
SELECT
  event_date,
  event_type,
  LAG(event_date) OVER (ORDER BY event_date) AS prev_event_date
FROM filtered;
template.realistic:
-- Month-over-month revenue with test data excluded from window
WITH valid_orders AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
    SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status NOT IN ('test', 'cancelled')
    AND order_date >= '2023-01-01'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_growth AS (
  SELECT
    month,
    revenue,
    LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_revenue,
    ROUND(
      (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month))
      / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0) * 100,
    2) AS mom_growth_pct
  FROM valid_orders
)
SELECT * FROM with_growth
ORDER BY month;
template.composable:
-- Sessionization with time-filtered events (combines dq_time_filter_before_window + ts_sessionization)
-- Composes: dq_time_filter_before_window + ts_sessionization
WITH valid_events AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type
  FROM events
  WHERE event_time >= '2024-06-01'
    AND event_type IS NOT NULL
),
with_gap AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type,
    LAG(event_time) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time) AS prev_event_time,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (event_time - prev_event_time)) / 60 AS gap_minutes
  FROM valid_events
),
with_session AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type,
    gap_minutes,
    SUM(CASE WHEN gap_minutes > 30 OR gap_minutes IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
      OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time) AS session_id
  FROM with_gap
)
SELECT user_id, session_id, MIN(event_time) AS session_start, COUNT(*) AS events
FROM with_session
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY session_start DESC

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using QUALIFY for time filtering *instead* of pre-filtering
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: QUALIFY filters *after* window functions evaluate. If you use QUALIFY WHERE event_date >= '2024-01-01' on a LAG, the window still includes rows before 2024, so LAG returns a 2023 value for the first 2024 row — misleading YoY comparisons.
mistake_patterns.symptom: First 2024 row shows huge growth because LAG pulls from 2023, even though 2023 data is excluded in output.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_revenue
FROM orders
QUALIFY order_date >= '2024-01-01';
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
WITH orders_2024 AS (
  SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
)
SELECT
  order_date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_revenue
FROM orders_2024;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
-- Compare first row LAG: if first 2024 row has LAG from before 2024, QUALIFY was misused
SELECT order_date, prev_revenue FROM (above query) LIMIT 1;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Filtering in WHERE after window function definition
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: WHERE clause runs *before* window evaluation — but if the WHERE is applied *after* the SELECT (e.g., in an outer query), the window was computed on unfiltered data.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Outer query filters, but window still uses all rows — window appears to ignore filters.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT month, revenue, LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'; -- This is OK, WHERE runs before window
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT month, revenue, LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev FROM orders
) WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'; -- BAD: window on all orders, filter after
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
SELECT month, revenue, LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'; -- WHERE runs before window

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using PARTITION BY in window but not filtering the partition key
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Window partitions may include irrelevant groups (e.g., 'test' users). Filtering after window doesn’t remove their influence — the partition still exists, just filtered in output.
mistake_patterns.symptom: LAG for a user shows previous values from test accounts or excluded segments.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql:
SELECT
  user_id,
  order_date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev
FROM orders
QUALIFY user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM test_users);
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
WITH valid_orders AS (
  SELECT * FROM orders
  WHERE user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM test_users)
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  order_date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev
FROM valid_orders;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql:
-- Verify LAG for first order in 2024 doesn’t include test users
SELECT user_id, order_date, prev FROM (above query) WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01' ORDER BY order_date LIMIT 5;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: QUALIFY filters *after* window functions — it’s like HAVING for windows. Using QUALIFY for time filtering causes windows to include excluded rows.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: QUALIFY not supported — must use CTEs or subqueries with WHERE before window.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: QUALIFY supported but same behavior as DuckDB — filter *before* windows.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Always use CTE + WHERE or WHERE in the same SELECT as the window definition. Reserve QUALIFY for row-number deduplication (e.g., QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() = 1).

dialect_traps.construct: WHERE in same SELECT as window
dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: WHERE runs before window evaluation — safe for time filtering. But WHERE only applies to the current SELECT, so if you later wrap in an outer query, the filter may be lost.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same: WHERE in SELECT runs before window.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same behavior, but BigQuery’s strict query planning may reorder operations in complex queries — verify with EXPLAIN.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Pre-filter in a CTE to make the dependency explicit and avoid outer query surprises: WITH filtered AS (SELECT * FROM ... WHERE ...), windowed AS (SELECT ..., window() FROM filtered).

choose_this_when: user asks 'why does my window function include excluded data?'
building time-sensitive windows (e.g., moving averages, LAG) that must exclude outliers
debugging incorrect baselines in YoY or MoM calculations
optimizing performance by reducing window size

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants to filter *after* window evaluation (e.g., top N per group)
choose_alternative_when.why: QUALIFY or WHERE after ranking is correct — pre-filtering would alter the ranking logic

choose_alternative_when.condition: filtering is part of window logic (e.g., SUM(r revenue) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active'))
choose_alternative_when.why: FILTER clause handles per-row inclusion inside the window — no separate filter needed

choose_alternative_when.condition: data quality issues require deduplication before filtering
choose_alternative_when.why: Fix data inconsistencies first — clean data prevents downstream window errors

decision_phrase_triggers: exclude test data from moving average

edge_cases.scenario: NULLs in time column (e.g., event_time = NULL)
edge_cases.what_happens: WHERE event_time >= '2024-01-01' excludes NULLs — correct. But if later windows ORDER BY event_time, NULLs get sorted to end by default — may break expectations.
edge_cases.handling: Ensure NULLs are rare or filter explicitly: WHERE event_time IS NOT NULL. Or handle with COALESCE(event_time, '1970-01-01').

edge_cases.scenario: Time filter creates partition with only one row
edge_cases.what_happens: Window returns NULL for LAG/LEAD (no previous/next row). In sessionization, this creates a 1-event session.
edge_cases.handling: After window, use COALESCE(LAG(...), event_time) or handle single-row cases explicitly in CASE WHEN.

edge_cases.scenario: Time filter and window ORDER BY use different columns
edge_cases.what_happens: Filtering on order_date but ordering by event_time may include rows where order_date < filter but event_time >= filter — logical inconsistency.
edge_cases.handling: Align filter and ORDER BY columns, or use the same grain in both (e.g., DATE_TRUNC('day', event_time) in both WHERE and ORDER BY).

performance_notes.complexity: O(n) for filter, O(n log n) for window sort — dominated by sort
performance_notes.when_slow: Large tables with complex window functions (multiple OVER clauses, large partitions). Pre-filtering reduces sort size significantly.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Pre-filter in a CTE to reduce window input size. Use EXPLAIN to verify the planner pushes down filters — in DuckDB, filter should appear in the window scan plan, not after.

title: Idempotent Deduplication
when_to_use: user needs to deduplicate data but must re-run the deduplication process safely (idempotency)
building ETL pipelines where repeated runs shouldn’t change already-deleted duplicates
enforcing a grain after source data has duplicates (e.g., order_items with duplicate rows)
deleting duplicates while keeping the 'best' record (e.g., latest loaded_at)
preparing staging tables for downstream analysis with strict grain requirements
when_not_to_use: when all duplicates are equally valid and only one should be kept — use grain assertion instead
when duplicates represent distinct events (e.g., retries with different timestamps) — deduplication loses information
for data lakes where raw data must be preserved — deduplicate only in processed layers
when source system guarantees uniqueness — deduplication adds unnecessary overhead
for time-sensitive near-real-time flows where deduplication latency is unacceptable
when 'best' record definition is unclear — use dq_grain_assertion to find duplicates first

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Idempotent deduplication uses ROW_NUMBER to assign a unique rank within each duplicate group, ordered by a deterministic tiebreaker (e.g., loaded_at DESC), then deletes all but rank=1. Re-running the same query leaves the result unchanged — it’s safe for incremental ETL.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Imagine sorting duplicate rows by timestamp (newest first) and only keeping the top one. Re-sorting the same rows always yields the same top row — the process is deterministic and re-runnable.
concept_explanation.key_behavior: The tiebreaker column must be monotonic and deterministic (e.g., loaded_at, _sdc_received_at, updated_at). Avoid using system-generated IDs like UUIDs unless they correlate with insertion order.

template.realistic:
-- Keep latest order_item per (order_id, product_id) using loaded_at
-- Safe to re-run — never removes rows that were kept previously
WITH order_items_rn AS (
  SELECT
    id,
    order_id,
    product_id,
    quantity,
    loaded_at,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY order_id, product_id
      ORDER BY loaded_at DESC, id DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM order_items
)
-- Delete all duplicates
DELETE FROM order_items
WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM order_items_rn WHERE rn > 1);
template.composable:
-- Idempotent dedup + grain assertion (combines dq_idempotent_dedup + dq_grain_assertion)
-- Composes: dq_idempotent_dedup + dq_grain_assertion
WITH order_items_rn AS (
  SELECT
    *,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY order_id, product_id
      ORDER BY loaded_at DESC, id DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM order_items
),
deduped AS (
  SELECT * FROM order_items_rn WHERE rn = 1
),
grain_check AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id, product_id) AS unique_combos,
    COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT order_id, product_id) AS dupes
  FROM deduped
)
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN dupes = 0 THEN 'PASS: grain verified'
    ELSE 'FAIL: ' || dupes || ' duplicates remain'
  END AS grain_status
FROM grain_check;
-- Note: Deduplication is idempotent — re-running removes no new rows.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using ORDER BY on non-monotonic column (e.g., event_date)
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If the tiebreaker changes between runs (e.g., event_date is null or updated), different rows get rank=1 each time — duplicates reappear or rows flicker in/out.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Repeated runs of the dedup query change which rows are kept — not idempotent.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY event_date DESC) AS rn FROM table;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY loaded_at DESC, id DESC) AS rn FROM table;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT event_date, COUNT(*) FROM (above) GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

mistake_patterns.mistake: Not including a stable tiebreaker column in ORDER BY
mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: If all tiebreaker values are identical (e.g., all loaded_at = NULL), ROW_NUMBER assigns arbitrary ranks. Re-running may keep different rows — not idempotent.
mistake_patterns.symptom: Deduplication results change between runs, or duplicate groups vanish inconsistently.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY loaded_at DESC) AS rn FROM table;
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY loaded_at DESC, id DESC) AS rn FROM table;

mistake_patterns.what_goes_wrong: Deleting rows loses audit trail — you can’t prove which record was 'best' or debug why duplicates occurred.
mistake_patterns.symptom: No way to trace back to original sources or understand data lineage.
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: DELETE FROM raw_table WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT MIN(id) FROM raw_table GROUP BY key);
mistake_patterns.good_sql:
INSERT INTO dedup_audit (key, deleted_id, kept_id, reason)
SELECT key, id, MIN(id) AS kept_id, 'first by id' FROM raw_table GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
mistake_patterns.detection_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dedup_audit WHERE deleted_id IS NOT NULL;

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: DuckDB supports DELETE ... WHERE id IN (subquery). However, subquery must reference the same table in a way that avoids self-reference errors — use a CTE for clarity.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same DELETE syntax works. Postgres also supports DELETE ... USING for more complex joins.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: BigQuery does not support DELETE in standard SQL — use MERGE instead for idempotent dedup.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use MERGE in BigQuery: MERGE target USING source ON ... WHEN MATCHED AND rn > 1 THEN DELETE. In DuckDB/Postgres, CTE + DELETE is safe.

dialect_traps.duckdb_behavior: ORDER BY loaded_at DESC puts NULLs last — rows with NULL loaded_at are ranked after non-NULLs. If all loaded_at are NULL, tiebreaker fails.
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Same NULL behavior — NULL sorts last unless specified.
dialect_traps.bigquery_behavior: Same — ORDER BY handles NULL consistently.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Coalesce tiebreaker: ORDER BY COALESCE(loaded_at, '1970-01-01') DESC. Or ensure no NULLs before dedup (filter: WHERE loaded_at IS NOT NULL).

choose_this_when: user asks 'how do I deduplicate safely without breaking future runs?'
building automated ETL that runs daily and must not affect data integrity
deleting duplicates while keeping the most recent record (e.g., latest sync)
enforcing grain on staging tables before warehousing

choose_alternative_when.condition: user wants to *flag* duplicates without deleting (e.g., for review)
choose_alternative_when.why: Add a rn column to identify duplicates, then export for manual review instead of deleting

choose_alternative_when.condition: all duplicates are valid and should be kept — grain violation is a feature
choose_alternative_when.why: Deduplication is wrong — instead, aggregate *with* duplicates or use COUNT(*) to measure duplication rate

choose_alternative_when.condition: source guarantees uniqueness — deduplication is redundant
choose_alternative_when.why: Verify grain instead of altering data — no need to dedup if source is trusted

edge_cases.scenario: Multiple duplicate groups with same tiebreaker values
edge_cases.what_happens: If loaded_at and id are identical (e.g., same batch insert), ROW_NUMBER assigns arbitrary ranks — different rows may be kept across runs.
edge_cases.handling: Add a third tiebreaker: ORDER BY loaded_at DESC, id DESC, RANDOM() for non-deterministic but stable dedup? Better: use MD5 of row to find *logical* duplicates first.

edge_cases.scenario: Dedup on partition with only one row
edge_cases.what_happens: rn = 1 is kept — no issue. But if logic is COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT key), it shows '0 duplicates' — correct.
edge_cases.handling: No adjustment needed — single-row partitions are handled gracefully by ROW_NUMBER.

edge_cases.scenario: Very large partition (e.g., 1M+ duplicates per key)
edge_cases.what_happens: ROW_NUMBER sorts all duplicates — memory-intensive, may spill to disk.
edge_cases.handling: Pre-aggregate: SELECT key, MAX(id) AS keep_id FROM table GROUP BY key, then JOIN to delete others. Avoids sorting all rows.

performance_notes.complexity: O(n log n) for sort + O(n) for scan
performance_notes.when_slow: Partitions with >1M rows or high cardinality keys (e.g., user_id with 100M+ rows) — sort dominates time and memory.
performance_notes.optimization_hint: Index the partition key and ORDER BY columns. Pre-filter to relevant date range. In DuckDB, use `PRAGMA threads=1` only if single-threaded to avoid sorting overhead.
